perturbation.original_text: 1.3. "Distributor" means Siemens, its Affiliates, or any Third Party which has been granted distribution rights whose scope includes the Products and/or Services by Siemens.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3. "Distributor" means Siemens, its Affiliates, or any Third Party which has been granted distribution rights whose scope includes the Products and/or Services by Siemens. However, for the purposes of determining liability under this agreement, the term 'Distributor' shall only refer to Siemens AG and not its Affiliates or any Third Party.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of “Distributor” for liability purposes to only Siemens AG, the contract creates ambiguity and contradiction with the initial, broader definition. This could lead to disputes about which entities are responsible for contractual breaches or liabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to limit liability to only one entity within a group initially defined as 'Distributor'. Agency law generally holds principals (here, arguably Siemens AG) liable for the acts of their agents (Affiliates and Third Parties) acting within the scope of their authority. By trying to redefine 'Distributor' only for liability, the modification contradicts established agency principles and creates uncertainty about which entities are truly bound by the contract's liability provisions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified contract language, by redefining 'Distributor' for liability purposes, directly contradicts agency law principles. The law snippet states, 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent'. By limiting the definition of 'Distributor' for liability only to Siemens AG, the contract attempts to shield its Affiliates and Third Parties (acting as agents) from liability, which is in contradiction to the mentioned law snippet, thus contradicting established agency principles.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1. Independent Distributor. Distributor shall be and must at all times make it clear that it is an independent entity contracting with Accuray, and is not the employee, representative or agent of Accuray. Distributor does not have the ability or authority to enter into any legal agreements or obligations that would bind Accuray in any manner.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1. Independent Distributor. Distributor shall be and must at all times make it clear that it is an independent entity contracting with Accuray, and is not the employee, representative or agent of Accuray. Distributor has the ability and authority to enter into legal agreements or obligations that would bind Accuray in any manner, provided such agreements are pre-approved by Accuray’s legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: By granting the 'Distributor' the ability to enter legal agreements that bind 'Accuray', it directly contradicts the initial statement that they lack such authority. This introduces uncertainty about the scope of the Distributor's power and agency.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text denies any agency relationship, specifically denying the distributor any authority to bind Accuray. The modification grants the distributor the power to bind Accuray, contingent on pre-approval. This contradicts the concept of 'independent contractor' outlined initially and introduces an element of 'actual authority' (even if limited), which needs to be clearly established in the contract. Absent further clarification, this provision will cause ambiguity regarding the distributor’s agency status and the scope of their power, potentially negating the 'independent' nature of the distributor.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the distributor lacks the authority to bind Accuray. The changed text directly contradicts this by granting the distributor such authority, albeit with pre-approval. The law snippet defines agency as a relationship where an agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal, and agreements made by the agent are binding on the principal if within the granted authority. The changed text, therefore, introduces an agency relationship where none existed before, contradicting the initial assertion of the distributor's independent status. The need for pre-approval does not negate the fact that the distributor now possesses the power to bind Accuray, which is a direct reversal of the original contractual provision.

perturbation.original_text: 4.6.1. Scope of Warranty. Accuray will provide a warranty to each Customer that the Products will be free from material defects and perform substantially in accordance with the written Specifications provided by Accuray as reflected in the regulatory clearance at the time of sale for a period of one (1) year following Installation of the Products at Customer's facility, but not to exceed eighteen (18) months following shipment of such Products to Distributor ("Warranty Period"). "Installation" of the System shall occur upon completion by Accuray or the entity installing the System, as applicable, of Accuray's acceptance test procedure demonstrating that the System substantially conforms to the written Specifications.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.6.1. Scope of Warranty. Accuray will provide a warranty to each Customer that the Products will be free from defects and perform in accordance with the Specifications provided by Accuray as reflected in the regulatory clearance at the time of sale for a period of one (1) year following Installation of the Products at Customer's facility, but not to exceed eighteen (18) months following shipment of such Products to Distributor ("Warranty Period"). "Installation" of the System shall occur whenever the Customer deems the system ready for use.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'material defects' to 'defects' and defining 'installation' as when the customer deems the system ready for use, the contract broadens the warranty scope significantly and introduces subjectivity that can create legal uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text defines a clear standard for the warranty based on 'material defects' and a specific 'acceptance test procedure'. The modification by removing the word 'material' broadens this substantially. The UCC allows sellers to disclaim or modify warranties, but these disclaimers must be conspicuous and clearly understood. More importantly, UCC 2-316 deals with the exclusion or modification of warranties. By not defining installation based on an objective standard and giving the customer unilateral power, the modification makes the warranty exceedingly broad and difficult to enforce, potentially contradicting the implied warranty of merchantability under the UCC and creating an unenforceable warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The original contract defines warranty scope based on 'material defects' and a specific acceptance test, while the modified contract broadens the scope by removing 'material' and redefining 'installation' based on the customer's subjective assessment. The Law Explanation states that this modification could contradict the implied warranty of merchantability under the UCC and create an unenforceable warranty. While the snippets do not contain the UCC language, the LLM explanation clearly indicates that the changes move the contract away from objective standards. The UCC exists to bring uniformity to commercial laws, and this change reduces the objective nature of the contract. The 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition states that the contradiction arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.
